statement: You are given an array of positive integers and a number k. Find the maximum sum of any contiguous run of exactly k elements. The array can be very large, so recomputing each candidate sum from scratch will be too slow.
tell: maximum sum of any contiguous run of exactly k elements

whyNotOthers.two-pointers: Nothing is sorted and no pair is sought; a fixed-width range slides over the array instead of two ends converging.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: k is the width of the run, not a count of largest elements to rank with a heap.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: There is no take-or-skip choice per element; the chosen elements must be contiguous.
whyNotOthers.greedy: No local exchange argument exists; the answer comes from mechanically scanning every window.

statement: Given a string of lowercase letters, find the length of the shortest substring that contains every distinct character appearing in the string at least once. If the whole string is already the answer, return its length. Assume the string is non-empty and may repeat characters heavily.
tell: shortest substring that contains every distinct character

whyNotOthers.two-pointers: The two ends do not move toward each other with a sorted comparison; one boundary expands while the other only contracts once the constraint is met.
whyNotOthers.trie: There is no prefix structure to search; the task is about a contiguous span of one string, not a dictionary of words.
whyNotOthers.monotonic-stack: Order relative to neighbors doesn't matter here, only which characters are currently inside the span.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: There is no need to try and undo choices; a single forward pass with a shrinking window suffices.

statement: You manage a factory sensor log represented as an array of temperature readings. Determine the length of the longest stretch of consecutive readings where the difference between the highest and lowest value never exceeds a given tolerance. Readings can rise and fall unpredictably throughout the log.
tell: longest stretch of consecutive readings where the difference between the highest and lowest value never exceeds

whyNotOthers.monotonic-stack: Tracking the running min and max of a shrinking/growing span is close, but the core loop is still window-based, not a stack that pops on comparison.
whyNotOthers.two-pointers: The two boundaries aren't converging toward a sorted target; the right end always advances and the left only catches up when the tolerance breaks.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: There is no sorted or monotonic answer space to binary search over; readings are unordered noise.
whyNotOthers.greedy: A single greedy pick per step cannot verify the tolerance constraint across the whole span without tracking a window.

statement: Given an array of integers and a target sum s, find the length of the smallest contiguous span whose elements add up to at least s. If no such span exists, return zero. All values in the array are positive, so growing a span always increases its total.
tell: length of the smallest contiguous span whose elements add up to at least

whyNotOthers.two-pointers: Both indices move in the same direction as the span grows and shrinks, rather than starting at opposite ends of a sorted array.
whyNotOthers.cyclic-sort: Values are not guaranteed to lie in a bounded range like 1..n, so index-based placement doesn't apply.
whyNotOthers.greedy: Picking the largest remaining element at each step doesn't guarantee the shortest contiguous span; the whole span must be tracked.
whyNotOthers.k-way-merge: There is only one array here, not several sorted lists to merge.

statement: You are streaming characters from a live chat feed into a fixed-size display buffer of width k. At every point after the first k characters, report whether the most recent k characters read so far form a palindrome. Process the whole feed and output one boolean per position from k onward.

whyNotOthers.two-pointers: Checking a palindrome uses two pointers internally, but the outer problem is about maintaining a fixed-width span over a stream, which is the dominant structural pattern.
whyNotOthers.monotonic-stack: There's no need to discard elements based on an ordering comparison; the buffer simply slides forward by one position each time.
whyNotOthers.trie: No shared prefixes across multiple strings are being indexed; this is a single evolving span of characters.
whyNotOthers.fast-slow-pointers: There is no cycle or midpoint to locate; the data is a linear stream, not a linked sequence to traverse at two speeds.

statement: Given two strings, a source and a pattern, determine whether the source contains a contiguous span whose letters can be rearranged to exactly match the pattern. The source may be much longer than the pattern, and matches can start anywhere. Return true as soon as one qualifying span is found.
tell: contiguous span whose letters can be rearranged to exactly match

whyNotOthers.two-pointers: The two boundaries don't converge from opposite ends of a sorted structure; one advances while the other trails to keep a fixed character count.
whyNotOthers.trie: There's no dictionary of words to search character-by-character; only one pattern's letter frequency needs matching.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No combinatorial arrangement needs to be tried and undone; letter counts alone decide whether a span qualifies.
whyNotOthers.union-find: There's no notion of merging groups or connectivity between elements; it's purely a frequency comparison over a moving span.

statement: You are given a row of house prices and an integer budget b. Find the maximum number of consecutive houses you could buy without exceeding the budget if you must purchase every house in the chosen consecutive stretch. Prices are all positive and the row can be long.
tell: maximum number of consecutive houses you could buy without exceeding the budget

whyNotOthers.greedy: Simply buying the cheapest houses first ignores the requirement that the chosen houses form one consecutive stretch.
whyNotOthers.two-pointers: Although two indices are tracked, they don't start at opposite ends of a sorted array converging toward each other; they bound a shrinking or growing run.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: Each house isn't an independent include/exclude decision; the selection must be one unbroken run of houses.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: There's no monotonic predicate over a sorted index to binary search; the running sum must be tracked incrementally.

statement: Given an array of integers and an integer limit, find the length of the longest contiguous subarray such that the absolute difference between any two elements in it is less than or equal to the limit. The array is unsorted and can contain both large spikes and long flat stretches.
tell: length of the longest contiguous subarray such that the absolute difference between any two elements in it is less than or equal to the limit

whyNotOthers.monotonic-stack: Maintaining running max and min candidates resembles a monotonic structure, but the overall control flow is still an expanding and contracting span, not stack popping based on order.

whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: The subarray length isn't a monotonic property that can be tested with a single sorted comparison; it must be verified by scanning.
whyNotOthers.union-find: There's no need to group indices into connected components; only a single contiguous run is being tracked at a time.

whyNotOthers.trie: There is no set of multiple words sharing prefixes to organize; only one string's internal repeats matter.

whyNotOthers.union-find: There's no notion of merging elements into connected groups; each triplet is evaluated independently by its sum.

statement: You are given two arrays of integers, each already sorted in ascending order. whyNotOthers.monotonic-stack: There's no need to discard earlier elements based on an ordering comparison; both sequences are already sorted and just need interleaving.

statement: Given an array of integers already sorted in non-decreasing order that may contain negative numbers, produce a new array of the squares of each number, also sorted in non-decreasing order. Do this without sorting the squared values from scratch afterward.

whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no locally optimal single choice; the largest square could come from either end, so both ends must be compared each step.

statement: You are given an array representing the heights of vertical lines placed along a number line. Choose two lines such that, together with the axis, they form a container holding the maximum amount of water. Return that maximum amount. Lines can be far apart or adjacent.

statement: A vending machine needs to make exact change for a customer using a fixed set of coin denominations, where an unlimited supply of each denomination is available. Determine the minimum number of coins needed to make the exact amount, or report that it's impossible.

whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: Each denomination can be reused any number of times here, unlike a setting where each item is usable only once.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: Trying every combination of coin counts would eventually work but ignores that overlapping subproblems for smaller amounts make bottom-up computation far more efficient.
whyNotOthers.cyclic-sort: Coin values aren't a permutation of a known index range being placed directly; amounts are built up incrementally from smaller achievable amounts.

statement: A carpenter has a single rod of a given length and a price list showing how much each cut length of rod sells for, and can cut the rod into any number of pieces of any of those lengths, reusing the same length multiple times if it's profitable. whyNotOthers.sliding-window: No contiguous span of the rod is being resized directly; the best revenue for each total length is built up from smaller achievable lengths.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: Enumerating every possible sequence of cuts would eventually find the best revenue but ignores that overlapping length subproblems make bottom-up computation far faster.

statement: A snack shop sells energy bars in a few fixed package sizes, each with its own calorie count, and a hiker wants to buy packages, potentially several of the same size, totaling exactly a target calorie count while buying as few packages as possible. whyNotOthers.two-pointers: The package sizes aren't scanned from two ends of a sorted list; the minimum count for each calorie total is built up from smaller totals.

statement: A 3D printer farm can produce parts of several standard sizes, each consuming a certain amount of filament and yielding a certain profit when sold, and any size can be printed as many times as desired. whyNotOthers.backtracking: Enumerating every combination of print counts would eventually find the best profit but ignores that overlapping filament-budget subproblems make bottom-up computation far more efficient.

statement: A stamp collector wants to affix stamps totaling exactly a required postage value on an envelope, choosing from a small set of stamp denominations available in unlimited quantity from a sheet. whyNotOthers.cyclic-sort: Stamp denominations aren't a permutation of a bounded index range being placed by direct index; the minimum count is computed incrementally.

statement: A candy factory produces gift boxes in several fixed weights, each generating a certain profit, and can produce any number of boxes of any single weight to fill a shipping container up to its exact weight capacity. whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: This isn't a matter of ranking box weights and picking favorites; every achievable exact weight total must be evaluated to find the best profit.
whyNotOthers.merge-intervals: No overlapping ranges are involved; discrete box weights are combined by exact quantity to reach a target capacity.

statement: A construction crew is tiling a hallway of a fixed total length using a catalog of tile lengths, where each tile length can be laid down any number of times in a row, and wants to know in how many distinct ways the hallway can be tiled exactly end to end. whyNotOthers.sliding-window: No window is being resized over a fixed sequence; the number of ways to reach each length is built up from smaller lengths using every tile option.
whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no single best next tile to commit to when counting all valid tilings; every tile length must be considered as a possible next piece at each length.

statement: An investor can buy shares of a few different funds, each fund requiring a fixed dollar amount per share and returning a fixed expected-gain score per share purchased, and any number of shares of any fund may be bought. whyNotOthers.union-find: Funds aren't being grouped into connected components; each purchase decision is evaluated independently against the remaining budget.

statement: A print shop offers a menu of poster sizes, each with a fixed cost to produce and a fixed square-footage of paper used, and wants to know the fewest posters needed to use up exactly a given amount of paper in stock, with any poster size printable any number of times. whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: Any poster size can be printed multiple times toward the total, unlike a setting where each size could be used only once.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: The poster sizes have no sorted structure being probed by halving; every achievable paper total must be evaluated incrementally from smaller totals.
whyNotOthers.two-pointers: Poster sizes aren't scanned from two ends of a sorted list toward each other; the minimum poster count is built up for every paper total from smaller ones.

statement: A bakery sells bread loaves in a handful of standard sizes, each with its own weight and its own price, and a caterer wants to fill an order for at least a target total weight of bread while spending as little as possible, buying any number of loaves of any size. Loaves of a given size remain available in unlimited supply for the order.

whyNotOthers.greedy: Always buying whichever loaf size has the best weight-per-price ratio first can overshoot the target awkwardly, spending more than a different combination of loaf sizes would.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: Loaf sizes can be purchased repeatedly to fill the order, unlike a setting where each available loaf could be chosen at most once.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: Ranking loaf sizes by value and buying only the top one ignores that the target-weight constraint requires evaluating combinations, not a fixed top few.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: Trying every possible sequence of loaf purchases would eventually find the cheapest order but ignores that overlapping weight-total subproblems make bottom-up computation far more efficient.

statement: A university course catalog lists, for many courses, which other courses must be completed first. Given the full list of prerequisite pairs, produce one valid order in which a student could take all the courses so that every prerequisite is finished before the course that depends on it, or report that no such order exists.
tell: produce one valid order in which a student could take all the courses

whyNotOthers.dfs: Depth-first exploration with reverse finish-order bookkeeping is in fact one standard implementation here, but as a label it misses the problem's signature: the required output is a prerequisite-respecting order over every course, which is exactly the ordering-under-dependencies family rather than generic graph exploration.
whyNotOthers.bfs: Plain layer-by-layer expansion from one starting course doesn't account for courses needing several different prerequisites completed by others first.
whyNotOthers.union-find: Courses aren't being grouped into connected clusters; the requirement is a linear sequence respecting direction, and prerequisite edges are directional, not symmetric merges.
whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no single locally-best course to lock in early without regard to the rest; the whole dependency structure must be respected simultaneously to build the order.

complexity: O(V + E) time / O(V + E) space

statement: A build farm compiles software modules in successive rounds, where each round compiles in parallel every module whose imported modules have all been compiled in earlier rounds. Given all import relationships, report which modules are compiled in each round, and flag the build as broken if some modules can never be compiled because their imports form a cycle.
tell: report which modules are compiled in each round

whyNotOthers.dfs: Post-order exploration can produce one valid linear compile order, but it doesn't group modules into the simultaneous rounds requested here; round membership comes from repeatedly collecting every module whose imports are all resolved.
whyNotOthers.bfs: A round resembles a layer, but membership is defined by all imports being resolved, not by edge distance from a starting module; a module one edge from the start may only become compilable in a very late round.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: There's no need to search over alternative groupings and undo choices; each round is constructed directly from the modules that currently have no unresolved imports.
whyNotOthers.greedy: No single module is a locally best pick to commit to ahead of others; a round is the entire set of currently compilable modules, dictated by the whole dependency structure at once.

complexity: O(V + E) time / O(V + E) space

statement: A recipe automation tool models kitchen steps as tasks, where some steps cannot start until other specific steps have finished, such as a sauce needing to be reduced before it's plated. Given all such step dependencies, output one sequence of steps that respects every dependency, or state that the dependencies contradict each other and no sequence can satisfy them all.
tell: one sequence of steps that respects every dependency

whyNotOthers.union-find: Steps aren't being merged into symmetric groups; each dependency points from one step to another, and that direction must be reflected in the output sequence rather than collapsed into shared membership.
whyNotOthers.bfs: Steps can depend on several other steps finishing first, so expanding one step's neighbors at a time misses steps that only become ready once multiple unrelated branches complete.
whyNotOthers.greedy: Picking whichever step looks quickest to do next, without checking what still depends on it, can strand later steps whose prerequisites were skipped over.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: No step is being included or excluded to fit a capacity; every step must appear exactly once in the resulting sequence.

complexity: O(V + E) time / O(V + E) space

statement: A city's zoning office reviews permit applications where some permits cannot be approved until other related permits are already approved, forming a network of approval dependencies across departments. Given this network, determine a valid processing order for every permit that honors all such dependencies, or report that the dependencies loop back on themselves with no valid order.
tell: a valid processing order for every permit that honors all such dependencies

whyNotOthers.bfs: Some permits wait on several unrelated departments finishing their own approvals, so a single outward expansion from one permit cannot correctly place every permit in the full order.
whyNotOthers.dfs: A recursive pass that records permits in reverse finish order genuinely solves this, but that is the ordering technique's own machinery; classifying the problem as plain exploration overlooks its defining ask — emit a dependency-honoring order over all permits and flag cyclic contradictions.
whyNotOthers.union-find: Permits aren't being merged into shared groups; the approval relationships point in a direction that must be preserved in the output sequence, not treated as symmetric connections.
whyNotOthers.two-heaps: No running median or balanced split between two halves of values is involved; this is purely about respecting directional ordering constraints.

complexity: O(V + E) time / O(V + E) space

statement: A video game's skill tree lets a player unlock abilities, but many abilities require one or more earlier abilities to already be unlocked before they become available. Given the full list of these unlock requirements, produce a sequence in which every ability could be unlocked while never violating a requirement, or determine that the requirements are impossible to satisfy together.
tell: a sequence in which every ability could be unlocked while never violating a requirement

whyNotOthers.bfs: Unlock readiness isn't measured by edge distance from a starting ability; an ability one link away may only become available after a long requirement chain elsewhere finishes, so distance-layered expansion mis-orders it.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No trial-and-error search with undoing choices is needed; the valid order can be built directly from how many unresolved requirements each ability still has.
whyNotOthers.greedy: Unlocking whichever ability seems most useful first, without checking what still requires it to wait, can leave later abilities stuck behind requirements that were bypassed.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-unbounded: Abilities aren't being chosen repeatedly to fill some budget; every ability is unlocked exactly once, and the concern is purely the order of unlocking.

complexity: O(V + E) time / O(V + E) space

statement: A film studio schedules post-production tasks like color grading, sound mixing, and visual effects, where some tasks cannot begin until specific other tasks have wrapped. Because departments work in parallel, the studio wants the tasks grouped into successive waves, where each wave contains every task whose blocking tasks all wrapped in earlier waves, or a report that a circular requirement makes the schedule impossible.
tell: the tasks grouped into successive waves

whyNotOthers.dfs: Post-order traversal yields one valid linear schedule, but not the wave grouping requested here; which wave a task joins is determined by counting unresolved blockers, which finish-time ordering doesn't directly express.
whyNotOthers.bfs: A wave is defined by all blockers being complete, not by edge distance from one starting task; a task a single edge from the start can still land in a late wave when a long chain feeds it.
whyNotOthers.greedy: Scheduling whichever task looks fastest first, without regard to what still depends on it, can place a task in a wave before something it needed has wrapped.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: No fixed number of top tasks is being selected or ranked; every task must appear in exactly one wave of the final schedule.

complexity: O(V + E) time / O(V + E) space

statement: A large software monorepo defines package dependencies where each package may depend on several others, and those in turn depend on more, forming a deep dependency graph across the whole repo. The CI system has unlimited parallelism, so determine the minimum number of build stages needed, where each stage builds every package whose dependencies were all built in earlier stages, and detect any group of packages whose mutual dependencies make building impossible.

whyNotOthers.union-find: Packages aren't being merged into unordered groups; dependency edges are directional, and stage placement must respect that direction rather than just tracking which packages are connected.
whyNotOthers.bfs: Stage membership is fixed by all dependencies completing, not by edge distance from any starting package, so distance-layered expansion from a source assigns packages to the wrong stages.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No speculative choice-and-undo search over stage assignments is needed; stages fall out directly from repeatedly peeling off packages with no unbuilt dependencies.

complexity: O(V + E) time / O(V + E) space

statement: A university registrar manages thousands of courses across departments, each with prerequisite chains that can span multiple departments, and a student wants to finish every course in the catalog in as few semesters as possible, taking in each semester every course whose prerequisites are already complete. whyNotOthers.greedy: Front-loading whichever courses look convenient cannot shorten the plan; the semester count is dictated by the longest prerequisite chain, which only global dependency bookkeeping reveals.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: No subset of courses is being chosen to fit within a budget; every course must be completed, and the question is how the dependency structure compresses into semesters.

complexity: O(V + E) time / O(V + E) space

statement: An event-driven pipeline defines processing stages where each stage subscribes to outputs from a set of earlier stages and cannot run until every stage it subscribes to has produced its output. whyNotOthers.union-find: Stages aren't being merged into undirected groups; a subscription points from one stage to another with a direction that the run order must respect.
whyNotOthers.two-heaps: No two balanced halves or running median is being maintained; this is purely a matter of respecting directional readiness constraints between stages.

complexity: O(V + E) time / O(V + E) space

statement: A spreadsheet engine recalculates formula cells, where a cell referencing other cells cannot be recalculated until every cell it references has already been recalculated, and references can chain across the whole sheet. statement: You are given the recorded temperature for each day of a season. For every day, determine how many days you would have to wait until a strictly warmer day arrives, or 0 if no warmer day ever comes. Comparing every pair of days is too slow for the input size.

whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: The temperatures are unsorted, so there is no ordered search space to halve.

statement: A parking garage's height sensors record a bar height for each column position along a single row, forming a skyline of adjacent rectangular bars all resting on the ground. whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: No subset of bars is chosen to fit a capacity; the rectangle must use a contiguous run of columns, not an arbitrary selection.

statement: A traffic camera logs the height of each vehicle passing under a scanner gate, in the order they pass. statement: A skyline rendering engine is given the heights of adjacent buildings along a street, and for each building it must determine how far the view extends to the right before a building at least as tall blocks the line of sight, measured in number of buildings. statement: A factory quality log records a defect-severity score for each item on the assembly line, in production order. statement: A queue management app tracks the number of people waiting at each of several checkpoints along a hallway, listed in order from the entrance. statement: A social network processes a stream of friendship events one at a time, where each event links two existing users together, and after every single event the system must be able to answer whether two given users belong to the same friend circle. whyNotOthers.trie: There's no shared-prefix string structure involved; the users are just members that get grouped together as links arrive.

statement: A telecom company processes a running log of cable-splice operations, where each operation joins two junction boxes into the same electrical segment, and at any point in the log the company must determine whether two specified junction boxes currently sit on the same segment. New splice operations continue to be added over time, and segment membership must be checked repeatedly as splices accumulate.
tell: segment membership must be checked repeatedly as splices accumulate

whyNotOthers.dfs: Re-traversing the entire cable network after each new splice to answer a segment query would be wasteful when the network only grows by one link at a time.
whyNotOthers.bfs: A single layered expansion answers reachability for a static snapshot, but here splices keep being added and every query must reflect the segments as they stand at that moment.
whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no cost or value being optimized by choosing which splice to perform; every splice in the log must be applied, and the task is purely tracking resulting groupings.
whyNotOthers.monotonic-stack: No comparison against a running set of nearby values is involved; the task is grouping junction boxes together, not ordering values by size.

statement: A distributed systems dashboard receives a live stream of "server A can now reach server B" events as network links come back online after an outage, and operators repeatedly ask whether two specific servers are currently part of the same reachable cluster. Because links keep being restored over time and each question must reflect the very latest state, recomputing reachability with a full traversal for every question is impractical.
tell: each question must reflect the very latest state

whyNotOthers.bfs: Running a fresh traversal for every reachability question would repeat huge amounts of work each time a new link is restored, rather than incrementally updating cluster membership.
whyNotOthers.dfs: A single exploration correctly answers reachability once, for a fixed graph, but this graph keeps gaining links and questions interleave with those changes throughout.
whyNotOthers.topological-sort: Restored links aren't prerequisite relationships requiring an ordering; a link simply merges two servers into the same cluster with no direction implied.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No speculative choice-and-undo search over server states is needed; cluster membership is tracked directly as links arrive.

statement: A banking fraud team merges account records whenever two accounts are found to share identifying details, such as a phone number or a payment card, and this merging happens continuously as new shared-detail matches are discovered across an ever-growing customer database. At any time, the team needs to check whether two specific accounts have already ended up in the same merged group due to some chain of shared details.
tell: merging happens continuously as new shared-detail matches are discovered

whyNotOthers.dfs: Re-exploring the entire account graph from scratch after every newly discovered match ignores that groups only ever merge incrementally, which can be tracked far more cheaply.
whyNotOthers.bfs: A single expansion answers group membership for one static snapshot, but matches keep being discovered over time and a query can arrive at any point mid-stream.
whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no locally-optimal merge to choose between competing options; every discovered shared-detail match must simply be applied to grow the grouping.
whyNotOthers.trie: Account identifiers aren't being organized by shared string prefixes; the grouping is driven by pairwise shared-detail matches, not prefix structure.

statement: A city planning tool is given a list of proposed new roads to be built one at a time between towns that already have some existing roads connecting them in various clusters. Determine which of the proposed roads, if any, would be the first one whose two towns are already connected through some existing chain of roads, making that particular new road unnecessary.
tell: already connected through some existing chain of roads

whyNotOthers.dfs: Re-running a full traversal of the growing road network before considering each proposed road in turn repeats work that incremental grouping avoids entirely.
whyNotOthers.bfs: The network of towns keeps gaining roads as earlier proposals are accepted, so a single layered search over a fixed snapshot doesn't reflect the connections built up so far.
whyNotOthers.greedy: No cost or benefit is attached to the roads to optimize; the task is purely detecting when a proposed road connects two towns already joined by earlier ones.
whyNotOthers.topological-sort: Roads connect towns without any direction or precedence between them; there's no dependency ordering to produce, only a check for redundant connections.

statement: An interactive photo-editing tool lets a user click pairs of adjacent color regions to merge them into one, and immediately after every click it must display the pixel size of the newly combined region so the user can judge each merge's effect. Merges keep arriving as the user works, and every size readout must reflect all merges made so far.
tell: display the pixel size of the newly combined region

whyNotOthers.bfs: Recomputing the merged region's size with a fresh flood fill after every single click repeats work across the whole editing session; membership and sizes change only incrementally with each merge.
whyNotOthers.dfs: A recursive fill measures one static snapshot, but sizes must be reported continuously as merges accumulate, with each answer folding in every earlier merge without re-exploring the image.
whyNotOthers.cyclic-sort: Regions aren't a permutation of index values being placed at matching positions; they're groups that combine step by step as the user merges them.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: No subset of regions is chosen under a capacity limit; every clicked pair simply merges, and the task is tracking group sizes as that happens.

statement: An email provider processes a continuous stream of "these two addresses belong to the same person" signals derived from login patterns, merging the linked addresses into a shared identity group as each signal arrives. At any moment, support staff need to check whether two specific addresses have ended up in the same identity group, possibly through a chain of several earlier signals.
tell: merging the linked addresses into a shared identity group as each signal arrives

whyNotOthers.dfs: Recomputing group membership with a full traversal every time a new signal links two addresses discards the fact that groups only grow by incremental merges.
whyNotOthers.bfs: A single layered search answers membership for one fixed snapshot, but signals keep arriving continuously and staff can query at any point between them.
whyNotOthers.topological-sort: The "same person" signal is symmetric with no direction or precedence implied between the two addresses, so there's no ordering to produce.
whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no optimal signal to select over others; every incoming signal is simply applied to merge its two addresses into one group.

statement: A puzzle game lets a player click to remove bricks from a wall one at a time, and after each removal the game must determine how many bricks, if any, that were connected to the ceiling became disconnected and fell as a result, given that bricks can be linked to their neighbors and support can route through several paths before a removal. Processing each removal against the wall's current connectivity, which keeps changing with every click, is the crux of the problem.
tell: the wall's current connectivity, which keeps changing with every click

whyNotOthers.dfs: Re-checking ceiling connectivity with a fresh traversal after every single click would repeat enormous amounts of work across a long sequence of removals.
whyNotOthers.bfs: A single expansion from the ceiling answers connectivity once, but the wall's connections keep changing after every click, and processing removals in reverse as merges is what makes incremental tracking efficient.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No speculative removal-and-undo search over brick orderings is needed; each removal's effect on connectivity is determined directly from the current grouping.
whyNotOthers.linked-list-reversal: The bricks form a 2D connectivity grid, not a linked list of nodes whose next-pointers need reversing.

statement: A logistics company adds new shipping partnerships between warehouses one at a time, where a partnership lets goods move freely between the two warehouses and, by extension, any warehouses already reachable from them through earlier partnerships. Given this evolving list of partnerships, identify the exact partnership at which every warehouse first became reachable from every other warehouse, if that point is ever reached.
tell: the exact partnership at which every warehouse first became reachable

whyNotOthers.bfs: Checking full reachability with a fresh traversal after each newly added partnership would redo enormous amounts of work that incremental grouping tracks cheaply instead.
whyNotOthers.dfs: A single exploration determines reachability for one static graph, but partnerships keep being added one at a time and the exact moment of full connectivity must be pinpointed as they arrive.
whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no locally best partnership to select ahead of others; every partnership in the given sequence must be applied in order, and the task is tracking when full connectivity is reached.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-unbounded: No repeated selection of partnerships to fill a budget is involved; each partnership is simply applied once, in order, to merge warehouse groups.

statement: A conference badge system links two attendees' badges whenever they scan into a session room together, and the organizer's dashboard must show the live count of distinct social circles after every scan-in event throughout the day. Because events stream in continuously, each updated count needs to build on the previous state rather than being recomputed from all events so far.
tell: the live count of distinct social circles after every scan-in event

whyNotOthers.bfs: A full traversal could count circles for one snapshot, but the count is needed after every event in a continuous stream, and re-searching the whole graph each time redoes work that incremental merging avoids.
whyNotOthers.dfs: Recursively exploring components recounts everything from scratch per event; each merge changes the circle count by at most one, which incremental group tracking captures directly.
whyNotOthers.cyclic-sort: Attendees aren't a permutation of known index values being placed in matching positions; they're being grouped by shared session attendance.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: No ranking of attendees by some score is requested, only how many distinct groups exist at each moment of the day.

statement: A text editor's autocomplete feature stores a large dictionary of valid words and must, as a user types each character, quickly list every stored word that begins with the letters typed so far. The dictionary can hold hundreds of thousands of words, many sharing long common beginnings, and suggestions must update after every keystroke.
tell: list every stored word that begins with the letters typed so far

whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: The task isn't ranking words by some score to find the best few; it's retrieving every word sharing a given prefix, which depends on shared character structure, not ranking.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: A sorted list of words could locate a prefix boundary, but as characters are typed one at a time, walking a structure built from shared prefixes avoids re-searching the whole dictionary from scratch on every keystroke.
whyNotOthers.dfs: Once the prefix's location is found, listing matches involves a small exploration, but finding that location efficiently for every keystroke depends on a shared-prefix structure, not a general graph traversal.
whyNotOthers.cyclic-sort: Words aren't a permutation of known index values needing placement at matching positions; this is about grouping strings by shared prefixes.

complexity: O(L) time per prefix / O(N × L) space

statement: A spell-checker needs to support two operations repeatedly over a large word list: confirming whether an exact word exists in the list, and confirming whether any word in the list starts with a given sequence of letters. Both kinds of queries arrive in large numbers and must be answered quickly against the same shared word list.
tell: confirming whether any word in the list starts with a given sequence of letters

whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: A sorted word list can locate an exact word, but efficiently answering many prefix-existence queries benefits from a structure built around shared beginnings rather than repeated range searches.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: No ranking or selection of a fixed number of best-matching words is requested; the queries are yes-or-no checks about exact words and prefixes.
whyNotOthers.union-find: Words aren't being merged into connected groups based on relationships between pairs; each query concerns a single word's presence or prefix independent of grouping.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No exploration of multiple candidate constructions with undoing is needed; each query is answered directly by following the letters of the query itself.

complexity: O(L) time per query / O(N × L) space

statement: A search engine's query suggestion box stores millions of past search phrases and must return every stored phrase that begins with whatever a user has typed so far, updating the suggestion list after each new letter. Because the same beginnings are shared by huge numbers of stored phrases, scanning every phrase from scratch for each keystroke would be far too slow.
tell: every stored phrase that begins with whatever a user has typed so far

whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: Retrieving every phrase sharing a prefix isn't about identifying a fixed number of top-ranked phrases by some score; it's a structural prefix match across a huge shared vocabulary.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: A sorted list of phrases only handles static prefix lookups reasonably; but since the same shared beginnings repeat across huge swaths of phrases and queries stream in constantly, a shared-prefix structure serves each without full re-scans.
whyNotOthers.sliding-window: No contiguous subrange of a single phrase is being grown or shrunk; the task compares the typed prefix against many stored full phrases.
whyNotOthers.bfs: There's no unweighted graph of connections to expand outward through in layers; phrases are compared against the query by their shared leading characters.

complexity: O(L) time per prefix / O(N × L) space

statement: A DNA analysis tool holds a reference library of gene-sequence fragments, each a string over four possible letters, and must repeatedly check whether a newly sequenced fragment exists exactly in the library or whether it is at least a prefix of some longer fragment already stored there. The reference library is enormous, and countless fragments share long stretches of identical leading letters.
tell: at least a prefix of some longer fragment already stored

whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: Sorting the fragment library could support some lookups, but with an enormous number of fragments sharing long common leading letters, organizing them by shared prefix avoids repeatedly re-comparing full fragments for every query.
whyNotOthers.union-find: Fragments aren't being merged into connected groups based on discovered pairwise relationships; each query independently checks one fragment's presence or prefix status against the library.
whyNotOthers.dfs: There's no general graph or tree of arbitrary connections to traverse; the structure being searched is specifically organized around shared leading letters.
whyNotOthers.cyclic-sort: The fragments aren't a permutation of known index values to be placed in matching positions; the task is about matching shared prefixes among strings.

complexity: O(L) time per query / O(N × L) space

statement: An e-commerce site indexes a catalog of thousands of product names and must power a search box that, as a shopper types, shows all product names sharing the exact letters typed so far as their beginning. New product names are also added to the catalog frequently, and both insertion and prefix lookup need to stay fast even as the catalog keeps growing.
tell: sharing the exact letters typed so far as their beginning

whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: The goal isn't narrowing down to some fixed number of best-matching names by a score; it's returning every name that structurally shares the typed prefix.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: A sorted catalog could support prefix range lookups, but with frequent insertions and lookups both needing to stay fast, a structure organized by shared prefixes handles both operations directly.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No speculative construction of candidate product names with undoing steps is needed; the search simply follows the typed letters through matching stored names.
whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no single irrevocable choice being locked in per character; every matching product name sharing the prefix must be returned, not just one favored option.

complexity: O(L) time per operation / O(N × L) space

statement: A messaging app's contact search must, as a user types part of a contact's name, return every saved contact whose name starts with those exact characters, even when the contact list holds tens of thousands of names with many sharing identical first few letters. The search must respond after every keystroke without noticeably lagging.
tell: return every saved contact whose name starts with those exact characters

whyNotOthers.sliding-window: No contiguous portion of a single contact's name is being resized under a running condition; the query compares a typed prefix against many stored full names at once.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: The task isn't selecting a fixed number of best-ranked contacts by some score; it's returning every contact whose name structurally matches the given prefix.
whyNotOthers.two-pointers: There's no pair of indices converging from opposite ends of a name; matching is about following shared leading characters across many stored names simultaneously.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: A sorted contact list handles static prefix ranges reasonably, but with many contacts sharing identical first letters and lookups firing on every keystroke, a shared-prefix structure resolves each query directly rather than re-searching ranges.

complexity: O(L) time per prefix / O(N × L) space

statement: A telecom fraud system stores a huge set of known scam phone-number prefixes and must, for each incoming call, quickly determine whether the caller's number starts with any of those known scam prefixes. Millions of calls are checked per day against a scam-prefix set that itself contains many overlapping and nested prefixes.
tell: determine whether the caller's number starts with any of those known scam prefixes

whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: A sorted list of scam prefixes could support some range checks, but with many overlapping and nested prefixes and millions of daily lookups, organizing them by shared leading digits avoids repeated range comparisons per call.
whyNotOthers.union-find: Phone numbers aren't being merged into groups from discovered pairwise relationships; each call is checked independently against the stored prefix set.
whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no locally optimal prefix to commit to and stop checking; the structure must correctly determine matches across many nested and overlapping prefixes at once.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: This isn't about ranking scam prefixes by frequency or severity to keep only the top few; every stored prefix must be checkable against each incoming number.

complexity: O(L) time per call / O(N × L) space

statement: A password strength auditor is given a large list of previously breached passwords and must check, for each newly chosen password, whether it exactly matches a breached password or whether it begins with the same characters as a breached password up to some length, since that pattern also indicates risk. The breached list is huge and full of passwords sharing long common beginnings.
tell: begins with the same characters as a breached password

whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: Sorting the breached list supports some lookups, but with enormous overlap in shared leading characters and repeated queries against the same list, organizing passwords by shared prefix avoids repeated full comparisons.
whyNotOthers.cyclic-sort: Passwords aren't a permutation of known index values to be rearranged into matching positions; the concern is shared leading characters among arbitrary strings.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No trial-and-error exploration of candidate passwords with undoing is required; each check follows the given password's own characters directly through the stored structure.
whyNotOthers.dfs: There's no arbitrary graph of connections to explore; the traversal is specifically constrained to following one password's characters through a structure organized by shared prefixes.

complexity: O(L) time per query / O(N × L) space

statement: A file system indexing tool tracks millions of file paths and must answer whether any indexed path begins with a given directory prefix, so that a "list everything under this folder" query can be answered without scanning every path. Paths are added and removed from the index constantly, and enormous numbers of them share the same leading folder segments.
tell: answer whether any indexed path begins with a given directory prefix

whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: A sorted list of paths could locate a prefix range, but with constant insertions and removals alongside enormous shared leading segments, a structure organized around those shared segments supports all three operations directly.
whyNotOthers.union-find: Paths aren't being merged into groups from discovered pairwise relationships between them; each query checks prefix membership against the whole indexed set independently.
whyNotOthers.bfs: There's no general graph to expand outward through layer by layer; the structure being queried is specifically organized around shared leading path segments.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: The task isn't narrowing down to some fixed number of top-ranked paths; it's a yes-or-no structural check for whether any path shares the given prefix.

complexity: O(L) time per query / O(N × L) space

statement: A voice assistant's command recognizer stores a fixed vocabulary of valid command phrases and must, as each phrase is spoken and transcribed letter by letter in real time, determine as early as possible whether the letters so far could still complete into a valid stored command, or whether they have already ruled every stored command out. The vocabulary can be large and full of commands sharing identical opening words.
tell: whether the letters so far could still complete into a valid stored command

whyNotOthers.backtracking: No speculative construction of candidate commands with undoing is needed; the check follows the transcribed letters directly through the stored vocabulary as they arrive.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: A sorted vocabulary list could check some prefixes, but with real-time letter-by-letter transcription and heavy sharing of opening words, a shared-prefix structure gives an immediate answer at each letter rather than repeated range searches.
whyNotOthers.greedy: There's no single best next letter being committed to irreversibly; the check simply follows whatever letters have actually been transcribed against the stored vocabulary.
whyNotOthers.two-pointers: There's no pair of indices moving toward each other; the transcription is checked incrementally against stored commands sharing the same leading letters.

complexity: O(L) time per query / O(N × L) space

statement: A single conference room has been requested by many speakers, each wanting to use it during their own start and end time, and any two overlapping requests cannot both be granted. Determine the maximum count of non-overlapping requests that can be granted the room, without ever needing to know the actual combined free time of the room.
tell: maximum count of non-overlapping requests that can be granted

whyNotOthers.merge-intervals: No ranges are being merged or combined into a unified set of covered time; the goal is choosing the largest possible count of individually non-overlapping requests to keep, discarding the rest.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: Each request has no separate weight or value being traded off against a capacity; picking the request that frees the room soonest is always at least as good as any other choice, which a value-based subset search would treat as needing full comparison.
whyNotOthers.dfs: No graph of dependencies between speakers needs exploring; ranking requests by end time and scanning once is sufficient without branching exploration.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No combination of requests needs to be tried and undone; once requests are sorted by end time, committing to the earliest-ending compatible request at each step is always safely optimal.

complexity: O(n log n) time / O(1) extra space

statement: A single charging station can service one electric vehicle at a time, and a list of vehicles each want to charge during their own preferred start and end time window. Determine the largest count of vehicles that can be charged without any two chosen charging windows overlapping, given that once a vehicle finishes, the very next vehicle to start can begin immediately.
tell: largest count of vehicles that can be charged without any two chosen charging windows overlapping

whyNotOthers.merge-intervals: The task isn't producing a combined view of when the station is occupied across overlapping requests; it's selecting the maximum count of individually compatible, non-overlapping requests to accept.
whyNotOthers.two-pointers: The windows aren't scanned from two ends toward each other; the standard approach is a single left-to-right scan after sorting by finish time.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: This isn't about ranking vehicles by some score and keeping a top few; every vehicle whose window doesn't conflict with previously accepted ones should be accepted.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: There's no separate value per vehicle being weighed against a shared capacity; always accepting whichever remaining request finishes soonest is provably optimal without comparing value trade-offs.

complexity: O(n log n) time / O(1) extra space

statement: A cashier must give a customer exact change using the fewest coins possible, and the available coin denominations happen to form a canonical system where repeatedly taking the largest denomination not exceeding the remainder is provably optimal. Determine the minimum number of coins needed for a given change amount under this coin system.
tell: repeatedly taking the largest denomination not exceeding the remainder is provably optimal

whyNotOthers.knapsack-unbounded: Coins of the same denomination can indeed be reused without limit, but here the coin system is specifically one where repeatedly committing to the single largest usable denomination is already proven optimal, without needing to build up and compare every smaller total.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No exploration of alternative coin combinations with undoing is needed; the described coin system guarantees the locally largest choice at each step leads to the overall minimum.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: The remaining amount isn't a sorted range being halved to locate a target; it's reduced step by step by subtracting the largest usable denomination.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: There's no ranking of a fixed number of top coin values to maintain; only the single largest denomination not exceeding the remainder is used at each step.

statement: A hiking group needs to cross a series of stepping stones to reach the other side of a stream, where each stone lists the farthest number of additional stones any jump from it may cover. Starting on the first stone, determine the fewest jumps needed to reach the last stone, given that reaching the last stone is guaranteed to be possible.
tell: the fewest jumps needed to reach the last stone

whyNotOthers.bfs: Layer-by-layer expansion over every possible next stone would also find the minimum jump count, but tracking only the farthest reachable stone at each step and committing to it directly avoids exploring every stone individually.
whyNotOthers.dfs: Exploring one jump sequence deeply before backtracking to try others wastes effort here, since always advancing to the farthest-reaching stone within the current range is provably never worse than any alternative.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No trial jump sequence needs to be tried and abandoned; the farthest-reach choice at each step is always safely optimal, so no undoing is ever required.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: No subset of stones is being chosen to fit within a weight or capacity limit; every stone along the greedily chosen path is used, and the goal is minimizing jump count, not selecting a subset.

statement: A delivery van has a single fuel tank and must travel around a circular route of fuel stations, where each station provides a certain amount of fuel and requires a certain amount of fuel to reach the next station. Determine the one starting station, if any, from which the van can complete the entire circular route without ever running out of fuel, tracking a running fuel balance across the route rather than testing every station as a candidate start from scratch.
tell: tracking a running fuel balance across the route

whyNotOthers.sliding-window: No contiguous subrange of stations is being resized to satisfy a running sum threshold; the answer is found by tracking a running fuel balance and only ever considering the station right after the first point that balance would go negative.
whyNotOthers.two-pointers: The stations aren't scanned by two indices converging toward each other; a single forward pass tracking cumulative fuel is enough to identify the correct start.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: The fuel amounts around the circular route have no sorted structure to halve; the valid start is found through one linear pass reasoning about where the running total first dips negative.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No trial starting stations need to be tested and abandoned one by one; the single running-total pass directly identifies the one valid starting station without exploring alternatives.

statement: A movie festival has many film screenings requested for a single theater, each with its own start and end time, and organizers want to accept as many screenings as possible so that no two accepted screenings overlap in the same theater. They want the maximum count of individually accepted, non-conflicting screenings, not a report of the theater's overall occupied time.
tell: the maximum count of individually accepted, non-conflicting screenings

whyNotOthers.merge-intervals: The organizers aren't combining overlapping screening requests into unified blocks of occupied time; they're selecting the maximum count of individually compatible requests while rejecting the rest.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: There's no separate value per screening being traded off against a shared capacity; the request ending soonest among remaining compatible options is always safe to accept, without weighing values against each other.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: This isn't about ranking screenings by some score and retaining a fixed top few; a request is accepted whenever it doesn't overlap with previously accepted ones, however many that ends up being.
whyNotOthers.dfs: No graph of dependencies between screenings needs exploring recursively; a single pass over requests sorted by end time is sufficient.

complexity: O(n log n) time / O(1) extra space

statement: A factory must assign workers to one-person tasks, where each task has a deadline and a penalty charged if the task is not completed by its deadline, and every task takes exactly one time slot to complete. Only one task can occupy any given slot, tasks may be dropped entirely at the cost of their penalties, and the goal is an assignment of tasks to time slots that minimizes the total penalty paid.
tell: a penalty charged if the task is not completed by its deadline

whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: Tasks aren't chosen to fit within a fixed weight capacity by trading off competing values; every task is considered in penalty order and placed into the latest deadline-respecting slot still open, without comparing subsets.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No trial assignment of tasks to slots needs to be tried and undone; committing each task, in penalty order, to its latest available valid slot is always at least as good as any other assignment.
whyNotOthers.topological-sort: Tasks have deadlines but no dependency relationships requiring one task to finish before another can start; there's no ordering constraint between tasks themselves to satisfy.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: The deadlines aren't a sorted range being halved to locate one target value; each task's latest available slot is found by direct placement, not by repeatedly halving a search space.

complexity: O(n log n) time / O(n) space

statement: A children's party has a supply of balloons that must be handed out to kids lined up for games, where each kid has a minimum happiness requirement compared to their immediate neighbors in line, needing strictly more balloons than a less happy neighbor. Determine the fewest total balloons needed to satisfy every kid's requirement relative to both neighbors, giving each kid at least one balloon.
tell: needing strictly more balloons than a less happy neighbor

whyNotOthers.dfs: No tree or graph of arbitrary connections needs exploring; two straightforward passes across the line, one in each direction, resolve every kid's requirement directly.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No trial balloon counts need to be assigned and undone; scanning left to right and then right to left, always assigning the minimum extra balloon needed relative to a neighbor, is always sufficient.
whyNotOthers.sliding-window: No contiguous subrange of kids is being grown or shrunk under a running aggregate condition; the requirement compares each kid only to its immediate left and right neighbor directly.
whyNotOthers.two-pointers: The two passes move in a single direction each, not toward each other from opposite ends; they aren't converging on a shared meeting point.

statement: A gas station chain wants to build refueling stations along a long straight highway, choosing from a fixed set of candidate spots, so that no stretch of highway longer than a fixed maximum distance is left without a station. Determine the smallest number of stations that must be built to satisfy the distance requirement across the entire highway, or report that no placement can satisfy it.
tell: the smallest number of stations that must be built

whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: Candidate spots aren't being chosen to fit a fixed capacity while trading off separate values; the farthest spot that still satisfies the distance requirement at each necessary point is always the safe choice, without weighing alternative subsets.
whyNotOthers.backtracking: No trial placement of stations needs to be tried and undone; always picking the farthest valid candidate spot when a station becomes necessary is provably at least as good as any earlier placement.
whyNotOthers.modified-binary-search: The highway positions do have an order, but the placement isn't found by halving a sorted search space for one target; it's built up by repeatedly advancing to the farthest valid candidate as the distance limit is approached.
whyNotOthers.top-k-heap: There's no ranking of candidate spots by some score with only a top few retained; the choice at each point is simply the single farthest candidate still satisfying the distance limit.

complexity: O(n log n) time / O(1) extra space

statement: A committee must select the maximum number of non-overlapping presentation slots from a long list of proposed slots, each defined by a start and end time, so that as many presentations as possible get a slot in a single-room hall. The optimal count comes from repeatedly picking whichever remaining proposal ends earliest among those still compatible with what's already picked.
tell: picking whichever remaining proposal ends earliest among those still compatible

whyNotOthers.merge-intervals: The committee isn't combining overlapping proposals into consolidated blocks of occupied hall time; it's selecting the maximum count of individually non-conflicting proposals while turning away the rest.
whyNotOthers.two-heaps: No running median or two balanced halves of values need to be maintained; a single sort by end time followed by one linear scan resolves the selection.
whyNotOthers.cyclic-sort: The proposals aren't a permutation of known index values needing placement at matching positions; they're time ranges being filtered down to a non-overlapping subset.
whyNotOthers.knapsack-01: There's no separate value per proposal being weighed against a shared capacity to decide inclusion; picking whichever remaining compatible proposal ends soonest is always safely optimal without comparing values.

complexity: O(n log n) time / O(1) extra space
